title: apexit
text: apexit Ends the application session immediately. apexit Copy Return Values None Use Allowed in Form, Report, and Service components, but not in self-contained components. Description The apexit statement ends the application session immediately. It has the same effect as pressing Control+C or Break. It is recommended that you use a close statement before apexit , to ensure all data is stored or updated. Terminating the application results in any triggers that would normally be reactivated (such as apStart) not being activated. If the component that issues the apexit statement has any child instances that were started with the newinstance (or with activate and an implicit newinstance ), these instances are deleted before the application session ends. Before they are removed, any Cleanup operation that is present is executed. Note: If this statement is executed while testing a component from within the Uniface IDE, the Uniface IDE is terminated. This is also true when you enter quit in the Debugger. The following example uses the apexit statement to exit an application from the quit trigger: trigger Quit askmess "Do you want to leave this application?" if ($status = 1) rollback close apexit endif end Copy apexit and finally If the code inside a try block of a try-finally-endtry construct causes the application to exit (using apexit ), further code execution stops because the current runtime context has been deleted, therefore the finally block is not executed. It is best to practice to avoid terminating the whole application using ProcScript. Instead, terminate all instances in a controlled way to allow Uniface to exit the application naturally when all instances are gone. If this cannot be avoided, place any module-related cleanup logic just before the apexit statement, and not in the finally block. Related Links break Unconditionally exit a loop initiated by for, forentity, forlist, repeat, or while. done Exit from the ProcScript module without changing $status. exit Exits the current component instance and returns to the previous or specified instance. return Exit from the ProcScript module, optionally returning a value to $status. finally Declares ProcScript inside a try-endtry block that must be executed, regardless of whether an exception occurs or not.
